title: Fortinet FortiClient EMS Pre-Auth SQL Injection CVE-2026-21643 (CVSS 9.8) Under Active Exploitation — Unauthenticated Attackers Extract Admin Credentials via Tenant-Routing HTTP Header Injection

sources.name: Help Net Security: Critical Fortinet FortiClient EMS bug under active attack
Bishop Fox: Pre-Authentication SQL Injection in FortiClient EMS 7.4.4

timeline.event: Fortinet PSIRT discloses CVE-2026-21643 and releases patch FortiClient EMS 7.4.5
Qualys publishes technical analysis of the SQL injection vulnerability
Bishop Fox publishes detailed technical breakdown of exploitation mechanics via /api/v1/init_consts endpoint
First confirmed active exploitation detected — attackers begin targeting internet-exposed EMS instances
Multiple security vendors confirm widespread exploitation; approximately 1,000-2,000 exposed instances identified via Shodan
Active exploitation ongoing; CISA KEV listing anticipated given confirmed active exploitation

simple_summary.what_happened: A critical security flaw was found in Fortinet FortiClient EMS version 7.4.4 — the software organizations use to manage endpoint security policies across their entire fleet of computers. The vulnerability allows any attacker on the internet to inject malicious database commands through a specially crafted HTTP header field, without needing any login credentials. The flaw was quietly patched by Fortinet in February 2026, but active attacks began six weeks later once security researchers published technical details of how to exploit it.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations running FortiClient EMS version 7.4.4 in multi-tenant configurations. Approximately 1,000 to 2,000 instances are directly exposed to the internet according to Shodan and Shadowserver scans. Affected industries include enterprises, government agencies, and managed service providers that use Fortinet's endpoint management platform to centrally control security policies across many devices.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker identifies an internet-exposed FortiClient EMS 7.4.4 server using search engines like Shodan or Censys. Step 2: Attacker sends a crafted HTTP request to the unauthenticated /api/v1/init_consts endpoint, injecting SQL commands into the 'Site' HTTP header field used for tenant routing. Step 3: Because the header value is passed directly into a PostgreSQL query without sanitization — before any authentication check occurs — the database executes the attacker's commands and leaks data in error messages. Step 4: Attacker extracts admin credentials, endpoint certificates, security policies, and tenant configuration data from the database. Step 5: Using stolen admin credentials, attacker gains full control of FortiClient EMS, allowing them to push malicious policies to all managed endpoints, steal additional data, or deploy malware across the entire enterprise device fleet.
what_you_should_do: Immediately upgrade FortiClient EMS to version 7.4.5 or later — this is the only complete fix
Restrict network access to the EMS web interface so only trusted administrator IP addresses can reach it (firewall or WAF rules)
Search web server access logs for requests to /api/v1/init_consts with unusual 'Site' header values — this is the attack signature
Rotate all EMS admin credentials and audit the database for unauthorized changes to security policies or new endpoint enrollments
Check Shodan/Censys to confirm whether your EMS instance is exposed to the internet
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: FortiClient EMS is the central control plane for endpoint security in organizations that use Fortinet products. A compromised EMS gives attackers the ability to push malicious configurations or disable security controls on every managed device simultaneously — essentially turning the organization's own security tool into a weapon. The attack requires no credentials and no user interaction, making it trivially automatable. With active exploitation confirmed and technical details publicly available, any unpatched internet-exposed instance should be treated as compromised.

tactics.description: Attackers exploit the unauthenticated SQL injection flaw in the publicly accessible /api/v1/init_consts endpoint to gain database-level access without any credentials
Attackers extract admin password hashes and credentials directly from the EMS database via SQL injection, bypassing normal authentication flows
Attackers query the EMS database to enumerate managed endpoints, applied security policies, certificates, and tenant configurations
With admin access to FortiClient EMS, attackers can push policy changes to all managed endpoints, effectively achieving lateral movement across the enterprise fleet
Attackers can deploy persistent backdoor configurations to managed endpoints via compromised EMS policy engine

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on identifying anomalous requests to the unauthenticated EMS API endpoint, SQL injection patterns in HTTP headers, and unusual database error responses. Network-level monitoring should watch for requests to /api/v1/init_consts from untrusted IP ranges, while host-based monitoring should look for PostgreSQL error spikes and unexpected admin account activity.

log_sources.source: FortiClient EMS Web Server Access Logs
log_sources.description: Monitor for HTTP requests to /api/v1/init_consts with unusual or long 'Site' header values containing SQL metacharacters
Watch for SQL syntax errors triggered by injected payloads — error messages in responses are the key exploitation feedback mechanism
Track all inbound connections to the EMS web interface; alert on requests from non-whitelisted IP ranges
Monitor for unexpected admin account creation, policy changes, or new endpoint enrollments that did not originate from known administrators

what_to_look_for.indicator: HTTP requests to /api/v1/init_consts with abnormal 'Site' header
what_to_look_for.description: The primary attack vector — attackers inject SQL into the Site HTTP header used for tenant routing in multi-tenant EMS deployments

what_to_look_for.indicator: SQL metacharacters in HTTP header fields
what_to_look_for.description: Presence of UNION SELECT, OR 1=1, single quotes, or double-dash comment sequences in any HTTP header values

what_to_look_for.indicator: High volume of requests to /api/v1/init_consts from a single IP
what_to_look_for.description: Automated exploitation tools will send many rapid requests to enumerate the database schema — rate spikes from single sources are a strong indicator

what_to_look_for.indicator: PostgreSQL error messages in HTTP responses
what_to_look_for.description: Successful SQL injection causes the server to return database error messages containing table names, query fragments, or data — watch for 500-series responses with DB error content

immediate_actions.action: Upgrade FortiClient EMS to 7.4.5 or later immediately
immediate_actions.description: FortiClient EMS 7.4.5 contains the only complete fix for CVE-2026-21643. Upgrade is mandatory — there are no configuration workarounds that fully protect against this pre-authentication SQL injection flaw.

immediate_actions.action: Restrict EMS web interface access via firewall rules
immediate_actions.description: Block all inbound access to the EMS web interface (TCP 443/8443) from untrusted networks. Allow only whitelisted administrator IP ranges. This reduces the attack surface even before patching.
immediate_actions.command: iptables -I INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -s <trusted_admin_cidr> -j ACCEPT && iptables -I INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j DROP

immediate_actions.action: Audit EMS logs for exploitation indicators
immediate_actions.description: Review web server access logs for requests to /api/v1/init_consts with unusual 'Site' header values, and check PostgreSQL logs for SQL error spikes. If found, treat the instance as compromised.

immediate_actions.action: Rotate all admin credentials and audit policy changes
immediate_actions.description: If exploitation is suspected, rotate all EMS administrator credentials immediately. Review the full audit log for unauthorized policy deployments, new endpoint enrollments, or configuration changes since February 6, 2026.

immediate_actions.action: Verify internet exposure via Shodan or Censys
immediate_actions.description: Search for your organization's EMS server IP/hostname on Shodan and Censys to confirm whether the interface is publicly accessible. Internet-exposed unpatched instances should be treated as compromised pending forensic review.
